---
title: "Modul 4 — Adopt-a-Gene (interactive)"
subtitle: "Bioinformatik NPLB24004U · runs in your browser, no install"
format:
  live-html:
    toc: true
    css: editor-theme.css
    include-after-body:
      - tutor.html
      - save-work.html
      - external-links.html
      - cynet.html
      - dossier.html
    resources:
      - data
      - cytoscape.min.js
engine: knitr
live:
  show-hints: true
  show-solutions: true
---

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

# Welcome

This page **runs R live in your browser** (via webR) — nothing to install. First you'll learn the core RNA-seq skills — **normalization and exploration (PCA & clustering)** — then you *adopt* a secondary-cell-wall gene and carry it through **co-expression → network → enrichment → interpretation**, building a *gene dossier*.

**How it works**

- Edit the code where you see `______`, then press **Run** (▶). Cells share state, so run them **top to bottom**.
- Most code cells are **commented line-by-line**, so you can follow the R even if a function is new. A few Week-2 cells use pre-written **"black-box" helper functions** (e.g. betweenness, shortest paths) that you only need to *call*, not read — those are labelled as such.
- When a cell has a blank to fill, **running it prints ✅ if your answer is valid, or a hint** — no separate step.
- The heavy upstream steps (kallisto read-mapping, DESeq2) were **pre-computed** for you — this page loads the resulting tables **automatically when it opens**, so everything is instant. (A few setup cells are hidden to keep things tidy.)
- A floating **📋 Dossier** panel (bottom-left) collects your findings as you go — it **autosaves**, you can **download** it, and **"Check with AI"** gives formative feedback (no answers — it coaches). The dossier is your **assessed deliverable** for the conference.

> The expression matrix, protein-interaction edges and GO sets are **real** (Klepikova *Arabidopsis* atlas E-MTAB-7978, BioGRID, GO). The differential-expression table is a small **illustrative example** (the real one comes from the server pipeline — see the teacher guide).

---

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
#| echo: false
#| edit: false
#| output: false
# (hidden setup — runs automatically on page load)
# read.csv(path): read a comma-separated file into a data.frame (a table)
# check.names = FALSE keeps the organ column names exactly as written
m         <- read.csv("data/expression_matrix.csv", check.names = FALSE)  # genes × organ samples (TPM)
info      <- read.csv("data/gene_info.csv")        # gene_id, symbol, description
ppi       <- read.csv("data/ppi_edges.csv")        # protein–protein interactions (BioGRID)
gene_sets <- read.csv("data/gene_sets.csv")        # GO term → gene memberships
de        <- read.csv("data/de_results.csv")       # example DESeq2 result
grn       <- read.csv("data/scw_grn_edges.csv")    # curated secondary-cell-wall regulatory edges (MYB46 GRN)

organ_cols <- setdiff(names(m), "gene_id")          # every column except gene_id = the samples
EXPR <- as.matrix(m[, organ_cols])                  # numeric matrix (needed for cor()/apply())
rownames(EXPR) <- m$gene_id
desc <- setNames(info$description, info$gene_id)     # quick lookup: gene_id -> description
sym  <- setNames(info$symbol,      info$gene_id)
# Seed any GRN-only genes (not in gene_info) with their curated symbol, so the network graphs show
# names (MYB58, VND6, ...) instead of raw AGI codes. gene_info stays authoritative — fill only the gaps.
grn_syms <- c(setNames(grn$source_symbol, grn$source_AGI), setNames(grn$target_symbol, grn$target_AGI))
grn_syms <- grn_syms[!duplicated(names(grn_syms))]
miss <- setdiff(names(grn_syms), names(sym)); sym[miss] <- grn_syms[miss]
say  <- function(cond, good, bad)                   # tiny ✅/❌ feedback helper
  cat(if (isTRUE(cond)) paste("✅", good) else paste("❌", bad), "\n")

# draw_heatmap(): a small labelled heatmap (genes as rows, samples as columns).
# diverging = TRUE uses a blue–white–red scale centred at 0 (for z-scores).
draw_heatmap <- function(mat, main = "", diverging = FALSE){
  mat <- as.matrix(mat); ng <- nrow(mat); ns <- ncol(mat)
  if (diverging){                                    # symmetric scale around 0 (z-scores)
    pal <- hcl.colors(50, "Blue-Red 3")
    lim <- max(abs(mat), na.rm = TRUE) + 1e-9
    idx <- ceiling((mat + lim) / (2 * lim) * length(pal))
  } else {                                            # sequential scale (counts/TPM)
    pal <- hcl.colors(50, "YlGnBu", rev = TRUE)
    rng <- range(mat, na.rm = TRUE)
    idx <- ceiling((mat - rng[1]) / (diff(rng) + 1e-9) * length(pal))
  }
  idx[idx < 1] <- 1; idx[idx > length(pal)] <- length(pal)   # clamp (keeps idx a matrix)
  fmt <- function(v){                                 # compact cell label across any scale
    if (!is.finite(v)) return("")
    if (v == 0) return("0")
    if (abs(v) >= 1000) return(paste0(formatC(round(v / 1000), format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ","), "k"))
    if (abs(v) >= 100)  return(formatC(round(v), format = "f", digits = 0))
    formatC(signif(v, 2), format = "g")
  }
  op <- par(mar = c(5, 6, 3, 1)); on.exit(par(op))
  plot(NA, xlim = c(0.5, ns + 0.5), ylim = c(0.5, ng + 0.5),
       axes = FALSE, xlab = "", ylab = "", main = main)
  for (i in 1:ng) for (j in 1:ns){
    yy <- ng - i + 1                                 # row 1 (Gene1) drawn at the top
    cellcol <- pal[idx[i, j]]
    rect(j - 0.5, yy - 0.5, j + 0.5, yy + 0.5, col = cellcol, border = "white")
    rr <- col2rgb(cellcol)                            # readable text colour for this cell
    lum <- 0.299 * rr[1] + 0.587 * rr[2] + 0.114 * rr[3]
    text(j, yy, fmt(mat[i, j]), cex = 0.65, col = if (lum < 140) "white" else "black")
  }
  axis(1, at = 1:ns, labels = colnames(mat), las = 2, tick = FALSE, cex.axis = 0.8)
  axis(2, at = ng:1, labels = rownames(mat), las = 1, tick = FALSE, cex.axis = 0.8)
}

# draw_network(): a tiny base-R network drawer (no igraph). edges = data.frame(source, target[, type]).
# Force-directed (Fruchterman–Reingold) layout; edge colour by type; GRN edges drawn as directed arrows.
# If there are too many nodes it keeps the hub + EVERY regulatory (GRN) node, then the highest-degree rest,
# so the regulatory layer is never dropped.
draw_network <- function(edges, hub = NULL, main = "", max_nodes = 44,
                         node_col = NULL, node_cex = NULL, node_border = NULL, node_lwd = NULL){
  edges <- edges[as.character(edges$source) != as.character(edges$target), , drop = FALSE]   # no self-loops
  if (nrow(edges) == 0){ plot.new(); title(main); text(0.5, 0.5, "no edges yet"); return(invisible()) }
  edges$source <- as.character(edges$source); edges$target <- as.character(edges$target)
  etype <- if ("type" %in% names(edges)) as.character(edges$type) else rep("coexpression", nrow(edges))
  isgrn <- grepl("^GRN", etype)
  capped <- FALSE; nodes <- unique(c(edges$source, edges$target))
  if (length(nodes) > max_nodes){                              # always keep hub + all GRN nodes, then top-degree
    must <- intersect(unique(c(if (!is.null(hub)) hub, edges$source[isgrn], edges$target[isgrn])), nodes)
    deg  <- sort(table(c(edges$source, edges$target)), decreasing = TRUE)
    keep <- c(must, head(setdiff(names(deg), must), max(0, max_nodes - length(must))))
    sel  <- edges$source %in% keep & edges$target %in% keep
    edges <- edges[sel, , drop = FALSE]; etype <- etype[sel]; isgrn <- isgrn[sel]
    nodes <- unique(c(edges$source, edges$target)); capped <- TRUE
  }
  n <- length(nodes); id <- setNames(seq_len(n), nodes)
  A <- matrix(0, n, n); ai <- id[edges$source]; bi <- id[edges$target]   # undirected adjacency for layout
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(edges))){ A[ai[i], bi[i]] <- 1; A[bi[i], ai[i]] <- 1 }
  set.seed(1)                                                  # reproducible layout
  x <- cos(2*pi*(seq_len(n)-1)/n) + runif(n, -0.02, 0.02)
  y <- sin(2*pi*(seq_len(n)-1)/n) + runif(n, -0.02, 0.02)
  k <- 1/sqrt(n)
  for (it in 1:250){                                           # repel all pairs, attract along edges
    dx <- outer(x, x, "-"); dy <- outer(y, y, "-")
    d  <- sqrt(dx^2 + dy^2); d[d < 1e-3] <- 1e-3
    f  <- (k^2/d) - (d^2/k)*A
    fx <- rowSums(f * dx / d); fy <- rowSums(f * dy / d)
    fl <- sqrt(fx^2 + fy^2); fl[fl < 1e-6] <- 1e-6
    step <- 0.08*(1 - it/250) + 0.004
    x <- x + step*fx/fl; y <- y + step*fy/fl
  }
  pal_e <- c(coexpression = "grey72", PPI = "#2c7fb8", GRN_activates = "forestgreen",
             GRN_represses = "firebrick", GRN_induces = "forestgreen")
  ecol <- pal_e[etype]; ecol[is.na(ecol)] <- "grey72"
  elty <- ifelse(etype == "GRN_induces", 2, 1)
  op <- par(mar = c(1, 1, 3, 1)); on.exit(par(op))
  rng <- range(c(x, y)); pad <- 0.12*diff(rng) + 1e-6
  plot(NA, xlim = rng + c(-pad, pad), ylim = rng + c(-pad, pad), asp = 1, axes = FALSE,
       xlab = "", ylab = "", main = if (capped) paste0(main, "  (", n, " nodes shown)") else main)
  ax <- x[ai]; ay <- y[ai]; bx <- x[bi]; by <- y[bi]
  sh <- 0.045*diff(rng); ux <- bx-ax; uy <- by-ay; L <- sqrt(ux^2+uy^2); L[L < 1e-6] <- 1e-6
  for (i in which(!isgrn))                                     # co-expression / PPI as plain segments
    segments(ax[i], ay[i], bx[i], by[i], col = ecol[i], lwd = 1.5, lty = elty[i])
  for (i in which(isgrn))                                      # regulatory edges as arrows, drawn on top
    suppressWarnings(arrows(ax[i]+sh*ux[i]/L[i], ay[i]+sh*uy[i]/L[i],
                  bx[i]-sh*ux[i]/L[i], by[i]-sh*uy[i]/L[i],
                  length = 0.08, lwd = 2.6, col = ecol[i], lty = elty[i]))
  ishub <- if (is.null(hub)) rep(FALSE, n) else nodes == hub
  fillv <- if (!is.null(node_col))    node_col[nodes]    else ifelse(ishub, "gold", "white")
  cexv  <- if (!is.null(node_cex))    node_cex[nodes]    else ifelse(ishub, 3.4, 2.3)
  bordv <- if (!is.null(node_border)) node_border[nodes] else rep("grey25", n)
  lwdv  <- if (!is.null(node_lwd))    node_lwd[nodes]    else rep(1.4, n)
  fillv[is.na(fillv)] <- "white"; bordv[is.na(bordv)] <- "grey25"
  cexv[is.na(cexv)] <- 2.3; lwdv[is.na(lwdv)] <- 1.4
  points(x, y, pch = 21, cex = cexv, bg = fillv, col = bordv, lwd = lwdv)
  labs <- sym[nodes]; bad <- is.na(labs) | labs == ""; labs[bad] <- nodes[bad]
  text(x, y, labs, cex = 0.62, font = ifelse(ishub, 2, 1))
  present <- intersect(c("coexpression","PPI","GRN_activates","GRN_represses","GRN_induces"), etype)
  legend("topright", bty = "n", cex = 0.72, legend = present, col = pal_e[present],
         lwd = 2, lty = ifelse(present == "GRN_induces", 2, 1))
}

# cynet(): emit a network as a text marker that cynet.html turns into an INTERACTIVE cytoscape.js
# graph (drag / zoom / pan) — the same viewer as N4f, but showing the network you just built.
cynet <- function(edges, hub = NULL, id = "cy", title = "",
                  node_fill = NULL, node_size = NULL, node_border = NULL, node_lwd = NULL){
  edges <- edges[as.character(edges$source) != as.character(edges$target), , drop = FALSE]
  if (nrow(edges) == 0){ cat("(no edges to draw yet)\n"); return(invisible(NULL)) }
  src <- as.character(edges$source); tgt <- as.character(edges$target)
  ty  <- if ("type" %in% names(edges)) as.character(edges$type) else rep("coexpression", length(src))
  nodes <- unique(c(src, tgt)); grn_src <- unique(src[grepl("^GRN", ty)])
  clean <- function(s){ s <- gsub('[\\\\"]', '', s); gsub('@@', '', s) }     # keep JSON + marker safe
  lab <- sym[nodes]; lab[is.na(lab) | lab == ""] <- nodes[is.na(lab) | lab == ""]
  hubv <- if (is.null(hub)) rep(FALSE, length(nodes)) else nodes == hub
  pick <- function(v, d){ if (is.null(v)) return(rep(d, length(nodes))); x <- v[nodes]; x[is.na(x)] <- d; x }
  fillv <- pick(node_fill, ""); sizev <- pick(node_size, 0)
  bordv <- pick(node_border, ""); lwdv <- pick(node_lwd, 0)
  nj <- paste0('{"id":"', nodes, '","label":"', clean(lab),
               '","hub":', tolower(as.character(hubv)),
               ',"reg":', tolower(as.character(nodes %in% grn_src)),
               ',"col":"', fillv, '","sz":', sizev, ',"bc":"', bordv, '","bw":', lwdv, '}', collapse = ",")
  ej <- paste0('{"s":"', src, '","t":"', tgt, '","ty":"', ty, '"}', collapse = ",")
  cat("\n@@CYNET@@", '{"id":"', id, '","title":"', clean(title),
      '","nodes":[', nj, '],"edges":[', ej, ']}', "@@END@@\n", sep = "")
  invisible(NULL)
}

cat(sprintf("✅ Data loaded: %d genes × %d organ samples. You're ready.\n",
            nrow(EXPR), length(organ_cols)))
```

---

## Weekly schedule

Module 4 runs over **two weeks (uge 43–44), Tuesdays + Thursdays = 4 sessions**, ending in a **student conference**. **Week 1** gets your data clean (normalization → PCA/clustering) and you *claim a gene*; **Week 2** builds and interprets the **MYB46 gene-regulatory network** in Cytoscape for the conference. *(An optional **GO over-representation** appendix — the hypergeometric test — sits at the end of the page.)*

**Week 1 — uge 43 · clean data → what does my gene do?**

| Session | Lecture | Hands-on (this page) | Dossier grows by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 · Tue** | F2 normalization + F3 EDA *(F1 background = pre-reading)* | **Stage 1** Normalization + **Stage 2** PCA & clustering | clean log/z matrix + tissue clusters |
| **2 · Thu** | F4 analyses (DE, co-expression as guilt-by-association) | **Stage 3** Claim your gene + **Stage 4** co-expression neighbourhood + **Database D1–D2** *(optional: GO appendix)* | adopted gene + co-expression module + predicted function + DB cross-check |

**Week 2 — uge 44 · gene-regulatory networks**

| Session | Lecture | Hands-on (this page) | Dossier grows by |
|---|---|---|---|
| **3 · Tue** | **F5 networks & the MYB46-GRN concept** + Cytoscape walkthrough | **Network N1–N4** (co-expression → +BioGRID PPI → +curated GRN → hierarchy) + **Database D3–D4** | 3-layer network + hub/hierarchy + GRN verdict |
| **4 · Thu** | short **F6** multi-omic briefing (knowledge-graphs removed) | **N5** Cytoscape figure → **student conference** | finished figure + 1-page dossier + ~10-min talk |

**Exercises on this page:** Stages 1–4 (R, incl. co-expression) · **Database exercises D1–D4** (web) · **MYB46 gene-regulatory network N1–N5** (R + Cytoscape) · **Close the dossier** (conference) · **Appendix:** GO over-representation (R: grid panel + real data).

> **Scope:** the live in-browser R lesson is **Stages 1–4** (normalization → PCA/clustering → claim a gene → co-expression neighbourhood); the **database** and **MYB46-GRN network** exercises follow. **Functional enrichment** — the hypergeometric over-representation test (a 20×20-gene grid panel, then your gene's real GO terms) — is now an **optional appendix at the end of the page**. *(These replace the old generic co-expression/threshold/network/GO/DE stages.)*

---

# Week 1 — uge 43 · clean data → what does my gene do?

## Stage 1 — Normalization: raw counts → TPM → log → z-score

This stage builds the standard normalization pipeline one metric at a time, and shows the matrix as a heatmap after each step so you can *see* what every transformation fixes. The aim is for you to understand **why** each metric exists, not just how to type it.

kallisto gives you **estimated counts** per gene. You **cannot compare counts directly** — two things confound them that have nothing to do with biology:

- **gene length** — a longer gene catches more reads even at the same expression level;
- **sequencing depth** — a sample sequenced deeper has more reads *everywhere*.

**TPM** (Transcripts Per Million) removes both: divide out length, then scale each sample to a million. We then **log-transform** (to tame the huge dynamic range) and **z-score** (to compare each gene's *pattern* across samples). The *Arabidopsis* atlas you analyse in the next stages is already in TPM — here you'll see exactly where it comes from. But first: **always look at the raw data.**

```{webr}
genes   <- paste0("Gene", 1:12)
samples <- c("Root1","Root2","Root3","Leaf1","Leaf2","Leaf3","Pollen1")
# ONE table to play with: the 7 sample counts + each gene's length (bp) in the last column.
# matrix(values, byrow = TRUE) fills it row by row. Change any number and re-run — nothing else needs editing.
mat <- matrix(c(
#  Root1  Root2 Root3 Leaf1 Leaf2 Leaf3 Pollen1   length
   80,    85,   78,   2,    1,    3,    0,        1500,
   90,   100,   88,   5,    2,    4,    0,        2400,
    2,     1,    3,  70,   75,   80,    0,         900,
    3,     2,    4,  85,   82,   90,    0,        3000,
    0,     1,    0,   2,    1,    0,  120,         600,
   20,    20,   20,  20,   20,   20,   20,        1200,
    0,     0,    1,   0,    0,    0,    0,        1800,
    5,100000,    6,   7,    5,    6,    8,         300,
   10,    15,   12,  25,   30,   28,    5,        2100,
   40,    38,   42,  45,   47,   50,   41,        4500,
    5,     9,    6,  12,   15,   11,    4,         750,
    3,     5,    4,  30,   28,   26,   10,        1350),
  nrow = 12, byrow = TRUE, dimnames = list(genes, c(samples, "length")))

mat[, "Root1"] <- mat[, "Root1"] * 100   # simulate Root1 sequenced ~100x deeper (a whole-sample depth artefact)

counts   <- mat[, samples]               # the count matrix (genes x samples) — what we analyse and plot
gene_len <- mat[, "length"]              # each gene's length (bp), read straight from the table above

draw_heatmap(counts, "Raw counts")       # labelled heatmap (helper is pre-defined for you)
```

> **Look closely:** can you see any tissue pattern? You can't — **one cell, `Gene8` in `Root2` = 100000, swamps the entire colour scale.** That single spike is almost certainly a **technical artifact** — and it's exactly why you must *eyeball* the raw matrix before trusting any analysis. Furthermore, **`Root1` (the first column) has far more reads than the other samples** — another technical artefact, this time caused by **deeper sequencing** of that one library.

### Step 1.1 — Find the outlier

The aim here is to **locate the single most extreme value** and name the gene and sample it belongs to — one artefactual cell can quietly distort every mean, total and correlation you compute later. This also teaches **matrix indexing**: in R you read a matrix as `counts[row, column]`.

```{webr}
# In R a matrix is indexed counts[row, column]:
#   ROWS    are the genes   (row 1 = Gene1, ..., row 12 = Gene12)
#   COLUMNS are the samples (column 1 = Root1, column 2 = Root2, ..., column 7 = Pollen1)
biggest <- max(counts)                    # the largest value anywhere in the matrix

# From the heatmap, the spike sits in the Root2 column. Enter that COLUMN number:
col_num <- ______                         # <- blank: 2   (Root2 is the 2nd column)

# now find which ROW (gene) holds that maximum within the chosen column
row_num <- which(counts[, col_num] == biggest)
gene    <- rownames(counts)[row_num]      # row number  -> gene name
sample  <- colnames(counts)[col_num]      # column number -> sample name
cat("counts[", row_num, ",", col_num, "] =", counts[row_num, col_num], "->", gene, "in", sample, "\n")

say(length(row_num) == 1 && sample == "Root2" && gene == "Gene8",
    "correct — counts[8, 2] = Gene8 in Root2 is the outlier (a spike that also inflates that whole sample).",
    "the spike is in the 2nd column (Root2) — set col_num <- 2 and re-run.")
```

### Step 1.2 — Why raw counts aren't comparable

The aim of this step is to make the two confounds concrete *before* we correct them, so the normalization that follows feels motivated rather than magical. We print the per-sample read totals (sequencing depth) and the per-gene lengths — two technical factors that change counts without any change in real expression. **Run this** to see both in your toy data:

```{webr}
# 1) sequencing depth = total reads per sample (the sum of each column)
depth <- colSums(counts)
cat("── Sequencing depth: total reads per sample ──\n")
print(data.frame(sample = names(depth), total_reads = as.integer(depth), row.names = NULL))

# 2) gene length in base pairs (one value per gene)
cat("\n── Gene length (bp): longer genes collect more reads ──\n")
print(data.frame(gene = names(gene_len), length_bp = gene_len, row.names = NULL))
```

> Look at the totals: **`Root2` (~100,000 reads) and `Root1` (~26,000) dwarf the others (~300).** `Root1` was simply sequenced deeper; `Root2`'s total is almost entirely the `Gene8` artefact — and a single count that large is itself only possible in a deeply-sequenced library, so `Root2` is deep *too*. Both totals are **technical, not biological**. And gene-wise, **`Gene12` (1350 bp) collects more reads than `Gene11` (750 bp) at the *same* expression**, simply because it is longer. TPM fixes **both kinds** — length first, then depth.

### Step 1.3 — Compute TPM (length, then depth)

The aim is to remove the two confounds from Step 1.2 and end with **TPM**, the metric the atlas uses. TPM is two normalizations done **in order** — do them one at a time, each with its own check.

**Step 1.3a — divide out gene length (→ RPK).** Divide each count by the gene's length in **kilobases**. Fill the blank, then **Run**.

```{webr}
# gene_len is in bp, so /1000 converts it to kilobases. gene_len has one value per gene (per row),
# so it lines up with the matrix rows automatically.
rpk <- counts / (gene_len / ______)            # <- blank: 1000   (bp -> kb)

draw_heatmap(counts, "Raw counts — length & depth still in")
draw_heatmap(rpk,    "After step 1 (RPK) — gene length removed")

say(exists("rpk") && all(is.finite(rpk)),
    "RPK done — values are now per-kilobase, so a long gene and a short gene at the same true expression read alike.",
    "the blank is 1000 (bp -> kb): rpk <- counts / (gene_len / 1000).")
```

**Step 1.3b — divide out sequencing depth (→ TPM).** Now scale each *sample* so its values sum to **1,000,000**. Each sample's own total is `colSums(rpk)`. Fill the blank, then **Run**.

```{webr}
# sweep(x, 2, v, "/") divides each COLUMN by the matching value in v; * 1e6 scales to per-million.
tpm <- sweep(rpk, 2, ______, "/") * 1e6        # <- blank: colSums(rpk)

draw_heatmap(tpm, "After step 2 (TPM) — depth removed too (raw scale)")

say(exists("tpm") && all(abs(colSums(tpm) - 1e6) < 1),
    "that's TPM — every sample sums to 1,000,000 and gene length is divided out. This is exactly the form the atlas is in.",
    "the blank is colSums(rpk): each sample is divided by its own RPK total, then x 1e6.")
```

<details><summary>💡 Hint</summary>
RPK = counts ÷ (length in kb). Then for each sample, divide by that sample's own RPK total (<code>colSums(rpk)</code>) and multiply by 1,000,000.
</details>

> Look carefully at the TPM heatmap: you can **just** start to make out **Leaf1–Leaf3 sharing a pattern**, and **Root1 & Root3** sharing another — but **Root2 still looks odd**, because the `Gene8` artefact dominates it (normalization does **not** remove outliers). It's still hard to read, since a few large values dominate the colour scale — which is exactly why we next **log-transform** (Step 1.4) and then **z-score** (Step 1.5).

### Step 1.4 — Log scaling

The aim is to make a matrix that spans several orders of magnitude actually *readable*. Taking **log2(TPM + 1)** compresses the range — every doubling becomes one even step — so moderate and low-expressed genes stop being invisible next to the high ones; the `+ 1` keeps zeros finite (log2(0 + 1) = 0). **Fill the blank with `tpm`** (the matrix from Step 1.3), then Run and compare with the TPM heatmap above.

```{webr}
log_tpm <- log2(______ + 1)              # <- blank: tpm   (+1 so a zero stays finite: log2(0+1)=0)
draw_heatmap(log_tpm, "log2(TPM + 1)")

say(exists("log_tpm") && all(is.finite(log_tpm)),
    "log scale: the dynamic range is tamed, so the root / leaf / pollen patterns start to show.",
    "fill the blank with tpm (the matrix from Step 1.3), then re-run.")
```

### Step 1.5 — Z-score (compare each gene's pattern)

The aim is to compare each gene's *pattern* across samples, not its absolute level. A **z-score per gene** — subtract that gene's mean and divide by its standard deviation across samples — recentres every gene on 0, so the heatmap shows where each gene is relatively high (red) or low (blue) regardless of how strongly it is expressed. This is the standard transform behind clustering heatmaps, and here it makes the tissue specificity jump out.

```{webr}
# z-score each GENE (row): subtract that gene's own mean, divide by its own SD across samples.
# Subtracting a length-12 vector from the matrix recycles DOWN the rows, so each gene gets its own mean.
row_mean <- rowMeans(log_tpm)            # each gene's mean across the 7 samples
row_sd   <- apply(log_tpm, 1, sd)        # each gene's standard deviation
z <- (______ - row_mean) / row_sd        # <- blank: log_tpm   (subtract each gene's mean, divide by its SD)
z[!is.finite(z)] <- 0                    # a gene with no variation would divide by 0 -> set to 0
draw_heatmap(z, "Per-gene z-score of log2(TPM)", diverging = TRUE)

say(exists("z") && abs(mean(z)) < 1e-6,
    "z-scored: each gene now centres on 0 — Gene1–2 root-high, Gene3–4 leaf-high, Gene5 pollen-specific stand out clearly. But Root2 still looks off (see below).",
    "the blank is log_tpm:  z <- (log_tpm - row_mean) / row_sd.")
```

> **But look at `Root2`.** Even after z-scoring, `Root2` still doesn't line up with `Root1`/`Root3` — its whole column looks scrambled. That's `Gene8` *still* making trouble: because TPM forces every sample to sum to a million, the `Gene8` spike ate most of `Root2`'s budget and deflated every *other* gene in that sample (Step 1.3). A per-gene z-score recentres each gene on its own mean, but it **cannot** undo a distortion that lives in one entire *column* — so z-scoring did **not** rescue `Root2`. The only fix is to remove `Gene8` and re-normalize, which is exactly what Stage 2 does next.
>
> **Rule for your dossier:** *estimated counts → differential expression; TPM → comparing a gene across samples; log / z-score → visualising and clustering.* Never DE-test TPM.

---

## Stage 2 — Explore the structure: PCA & clustering

Now that the matrix is normalized, step back and ask whether the **samples** group the way biology predicts — and deal with the `Gene8` artefact honestly. We do it in the right order: **first see the damage** the artefact does to the global picture, **then** remove it and re-normalize, and watch the structure snap into place. *(Run Stage 1 first so `counts`, `gene_len` and `z` exist.)*

### PCA — first, with the artefact still in

PCA squeezes the genes into a few **principal components** that capture the most variation between samples; if normalization worked, samples should separate by **tissue**. Let's run it first on the matrix **from Step 1.5 — `Gene8` still included** — and see what one bad gene does.

```{webr}
# z is the per-gene z-score from Step 1.5 — it STILL contains Gene8.
tissue <- c("Root","Root","Root","Leaf","Leaf","Leaf","Pollen")   # label each sample
pca0 <- prcomp(t(z))                                              # PCA with samples as rows
op <- par(mar = c(4, 4, 3, 7))                                    # extra right margin for the legend
plot(pca0$x[,1], pca0$x[,2], col = factor(tissue), pch = 19, cex = 1.6,
     xlab = "PC1", ylab = "PC2", main = "PCA — Gene8 (artefact) still in")
text(pca0$x[,1], pca0$x[,2], colnames(z), pos = 3, cex = 0.7)
legend(par("usr")[2] * 1.02, par("usr")[4], xpd = TRUE, bty = "n",
       legend = levels(factor(tissue)), col = 1:3, pch = 19)
par(op)
```

> **What goes wrong.** `Root2` is dragged away from `Root1`/`Root3` — the three roots **don't** group. The `Gene8` spike, even after per-gene z-scoring, distorts `Root2`'s whole profile (TPM's sum-to-million crushed every other gene in that sample, Step 1.5). One bad gene is enough to break the global picture.

### Remove the artefact and re-normalize

Sometimes a gene — or even a whole sample — is so badly behaved that **no normalization can rescue it**; you detect it, document *why*, and remove it. `Gene8` is exactly such a case. We drop it and re-run the **same** TPM → log → z-score pipeline on the clean matrix.

```{webr}
# Gene8 was the artefact (Step 1.1). Drop it, then re-run the SAME pipeline — this is the compact,
# "pro" way an analyst writes the whole TPM -> log -> z-score in a few lines:
keep <- rownames(counts) != "Gene8"
crpk <- counts[keep, ] / (gene_len[keep] / 1000)          # TPM step 1: divide out gene length
ctpm <- sweep(crpk, 2, colSums(crpk), "/") * 1e6          # TPM step 2: divide out depth
clog <- log2(ctpm + 1)                                    # log
cz   <- (clog - rowMeans(clog)) / apply(clog, 1, sd)      # per-gene z-score (same row trick as Step 1.5)
cz[!is.finite(cz)] <- 0
tissue <- c("Root","Root","Root","Leaf","Leaf","Leaf","Pollen")   # label each sample
cat("Clean, re-normalized matrix:", nrow(cz), "genes ×", ncol(cz), "samples\n")
draw_heatmap(cz, "Per-gene z-score of log2(TPM) — Gene8 removed", diverging = TRUE)
```

> With the artefact gone, the z-score heatmap already shows clean tissue blocks — root genes high (red) in the root samples, leaf genes high in the leaves, and so on.
>
> **🔬 Task:** change the first line to **keep** Gene8 — e.g. `keep <- rownames(counts) != "none"` — re-run this cell, the PCA below, *and* the clustering. Watch `Root2` break away again. That's *why* you remove artefacts before analysis.

### PCA — after removing the artefact

Now the same PCA on the **clean** matrix `cz`. The three tissues should fall into three tight groups.

```{webr}
# prcomp(t(cz)): PCA with SAMPLES as rows (t() transposes). cz is already z-scored per gene.
pca <- prcomp(t(cz))
op <- par(mar = c(4, 4, 3, 7))                                 # extra right margin for the legend
# pca$x = sample coordinates; [,1]/[,2] are PC1/PC2.  factor(tissue): turn labels into colour groups
plot(pca$x[,1], pca$x[,2], col = factor(tissue), pch = 19, cex = 1.6,
     xlab = "PC1", ylab = "PC2", main = "PCA of samples — Gene8 removed")
text(pca$x[,1], pca$x[,2], colnames(cz), pos = 3, cex = 0.7)   # text(): label each point
legend(par("usr")[2] * 1.02, par("usr")[4], xpd = TRUE, bty = "n",   # just OUTSIDE the plot, top-right
       legend = levels(factor(tissue)), col = 1:3, pch = 19)
par(op)
```

> Compare with the first PCA: removing one gene and re-normalizing pulled `Root2` back in with the other roots. **That** is the payoff of `detect → remove → re-normalize`.

How much of the variation does each component capture? The **scree plot** answers that — and tells you whether the 2-D PCA picture can be trusted.

```{webr}
# pca$sdev = standard deviation of each PC; squared = variance. Divide by the total for a proportion.
var_explained <- pca$sdev^2 / sum(pca$sdev^2)
barplot(100 * var_explained, names.arg = paste0("PC", seq_along(var_explained)),
        ylab = "% variance explained", main = "Scree plot")
cat(sprintf("PC1 captures %.0f%% of the variance, PC2 %.0f%% — together %.0f%%.\n",
            100*var_explained[1], 100*var_explained[2], 100*sum(var_explained[1:2])))
```

> **What it means:** each bar is the share of total variation that component captures. Here a couple of tall bars (PC1–PC2) hold most of it, so the data is essentially **low-dimensional** and the 2-D PCA plot above is a *faithful* summary of the samples. If instead the bars were all roughly equal height, no 2-D plot could represent the data and you should distrust any clustering you read off it.

### Hierarchical clustering — which samples are most alike?

Clustering repeatedly joins the two most similar samples into a tree (a **dendrogram**); samples from the same tissue should join first. We measure similarity as the distance between samples in z-score space. The **linkage** is the rule for *how* clusters merge — and it changes the tree.

```{webr}
LINK <- "ward.D2"            # <- try also: "complete", "average", "single" — then re-run
d  <- dist(t(cz))            # dist(): pairwise distances between samples (rows of t(cz))
hc <- hclust(d, method = LINK)
plot(hc, labels = colnames(cz), main = paste("Clustering of samples —", LINK, "linkage"), xlab = "", sub = "")
```

> **🔬 Explore the linkage.** Re-run with `LINK <- "single"`, then `"complete"`, then `"average"`. **Single** linkage often *chains* samples into one straggly cluster (it joins on the single closest pair); **complete** and **ward.D2** give compact, balanced clusters — which is why ward.D2 is a common default. Note under each method whether the three tissues still separate, and whether `Root2` joins the roots or hangs off on its own.

**🔮 Your turn:** there are **three** tissues here, so cut the tree into three groups and check they match. **Fill the blank** with `3`, then **Run**.

```{webr}
# cutree(hc, k): cut the dendrogram into k groups (k = number of tissues here)
groups <- cutree(hc, k = ______)        # <- blank: 3   (root, leaf, pollen)

# colour each LEAF LABEL by its cluster, so you can read the cut straight off the tree
pal3 <- c("forestgreen", "firebrick", "steelblue", "orange")   # one colour per cluster
dend <- as.dendrogram(hc)
dend <- dendrapply(dend, function(node){
  if (is.leaf(node)) {                                  # only the tips carry sample names
    s <- attr(node, "label")                            # the sample at this leaf
    attr(node, "nodePar") <- list(lab.col = pal3[groups[s]], pch = NA)
  }
  node
})
plot(dend, main = "Samples coloured by cluster", ylab = "Height")
print(data.frame(sample = colnames(cz), tissue = tissue, cluster = groups))

say(exists("groups") && length(unique(groups)) == 3,
    "three clusters that line up with root / leaf / pollen — once the artefact is removed, the structure is purely biological.",
    "we have three tissues, so cut the tree into 3 groups.")
```

> Notice this only worked **after** removing `Gene8`: with the artefact in, its spike dominated `Root2` and scrambled the clustering — no choice of linkage fixes that, you have to remove the bad data. In **real** data you watch for **batch effects** the same way: if samples cluster by sequencing run instead of biology, correct for it (batch term in DESeq2, or ComBat) **before** drawing conclusions.

---

## Stage 3 — Claim your gene

You've got the skills — now make it personal. Pick **one** gene from the pool (your TA prevents duplicates):

`CESA4 AT5G44030` · `CESA7 AT5G17420` · `CESA8 AT4G18780` · `IRX9 AT2G37090` · `LAC4 AT2G38080` · `MYB46 AT5G12870` · `SND1 AT1G32770` · `COBL4 AT5G15630` · **decoys (NOT cell-wall — for you to expose):** `CAB1 AT1G29930`, `RBCS1A AT1G67090`

**Instructions:** replace both `______`, then **Run** — the cell tells you if your gene is valid.

```{webr}
MY_GENE <- "______"   # <- your gene's AGI locus, e.g. "AT5G17420"
MY_NAME <- "______"   # <- a short name, e.g. "CESA7"
cat("Adopted:", MY_NAME, "(", MY_GENE, ")\n")   # cat() prints its arguments

# instant check: grepl() tests the AGI text pattern; %in% checks the gene is actually in the data
say(grepl("^AT[1-5CM]G[0-9]{5}$", MY_GENE) && MY_GENE %in% rownames(EXPR),
    "valid gene, present in the data — onwards!",
    "not a valid/known locus — pick one from the pool above and re-run.")
```

<details><summary>💡 Hint</summary>
An AGI locus looks like `AT` + chromosome (1–5) + `G` + five digits, in quotes: `"AT5G17420"`.
</details>

**✍️ Dossier:** write your hypothesis — *genes co-expressed with my gene are enriched for ______, so my gene acts in ______.* *(Open the **📋 Dossier** panel, bottom-left, and fill the "Hypothesis" field — it autosaves and an AI tutor can check it.)*

---

## Stage 4 — Co-expression: find your gene's neighbours

Before you reach for a database or build a network, do the simplest, most powerful trick in transcriptomics yourself: **guilt by association**. Genes that rise and fall *together* across many samples are often part of the same process — so the genes whose expression profile most resembles your gene's are your first, data-driven guess at what it does.

We measure "resembles" with the **Pearson correlation** of each gene's profile against your gene's, then sort: the top of the list is your gene's **co-expression neighbourhood**. *(Run Stage 3 first so `MY_GENE` is set.)*

```{webr}
HUB    <- MY_GENE                                  # your adopted gene (from Stage 3)
target <- EXPR[HUB, ]                              # its expression across every organ
# cor(r, target): Pearson correlation of each gene's profile with your gene's profile
pcc  <- apply(EXPR, 1, function(r) suppressWarnings(cor(r, ______)))   # <- blank: target
nbhd <- data.frame(gene = rownames(EXPR), symbol = sym[rownames(EXPR)],
                   description = desc[rownames(EXPR)], PCC = round(pcc, 3))
nbhd <- head(nbhd[order(-nbhd$PCC), ], 60)         # keep the top-60 neighbours
print(head(nbhd, 12))

say(nrow(nbhd) > 0 && nbhd$gene[1] == HUB && round(nbhd$PCC[1], 3) == 1,
    paste("row 1 is", MY_NAME, "at PCC = 1.000 — neighbour list ready."),
    "row 1 should be your own gene at PCC = 1.000 — fill the blank with 'target' and re-run.")
```

**🤔 Read your neighbourhood — why does *your own gene* sit at the very top of its list, at PCC = 1.000?**

<details><summary>💡 Hint</summary>A gene's expression profile is identical to itself, and the Pearson correlation of any profile with itself is exactly 1.0 — so every gene is its own perfect "neighbour". (It's also a quick sanity check that the correlation ran correctly.)</details>

> Now look at rows 2–12: do they form **one biological programme**? For a wall gene you'll see a tight secondary-cell-wall module — other `CESA`s, laccases, xylan / `FLA` / `IRX` genes (the exact names vary, and not recognising every one is fine). **If you adopted a decoy** (e.g. a photosynthesis gene), you'll instead see a *completely different* module — and that mismatch is itself the answer to "is my gene really a cell-wall gene?"

**🤔 Your top neighbour correlates at PCC ≈ 0.95 with your gene. Does that *prove* the two proteins interact, or that one regulates the other?**

<details><summary>💡 Hint</summary>No — a high correlation only means the two genes share an expression <i>pattern</i>. That is <i>consistent with</i> a functional link, but it could equally come from both being switched on by the same upstream regulator, or both being specific to the same tissue. Proof needs <b>physical</b> (PPI) or <b>directed</b> (regulatory) evidence — which you add in <b>Week 2</b>, where you turn this ranked list into a real network and see where co-expression does and doesn't line up with mechanism.</details>

> **✍️ Dossier:** record your **top 5 co-expressed genes** (symbol + PCC) and, in one line, the process they suggest your gene acts in. *(Open the **📋 Dossier** panel, bottom-left, and fill the "Co-expression" field.)*

---

## Database exercises — the database half of your dossier (D1–D4)

Every number so far came from **one** atlas. Real evidence is a *consensus* across resources. In these four short **web** exercises (no coding — just clicking) you cross-examine **your adopted gene** in the major plant databases and fill **one results table**: the database half of your gene dossier. Each step also tests what you just built in **Stage 4** (your co-expression list) and sets up the **Week-2 MYB46 network**. All four are **assessed** (TA-checked against an answer key). *URLs verified 2026-06-19; all open in a new tab.*

**📋 Your database dossier — fill it in as you go (this table is what's assessed):**

| Field | Your gene | From |
|---|---|---|
| Protein domain (PFAM/InterPro) | | D1 · PEO |
| Biological function (MapMan) | | D1 · PEO |
| Highest organ | | D1, D4 |
| Root cell type | xylem? | D1 · ePlant |
| Top 3 co-expressed (databases) | | D2 |
| Overlap with my Stage-4 list (of 5) | / 5 | D2 |
| Physical (PPI) partners | | D3 · STRING |
| Regulator directly above my gene | | D3 · CoNekT |
| Top organ — agrees across 3 DBs? | yes / no | D4 |
| **One-line function + 3 evidence types** | | synthesis |

### D1 — What is my gene, and where is it on?  *(ePlant eFP + PEO)*

1. **ePlant** ([bar.utoronto.ca/eplant](https://bar.utoronto.ca/eplant/)) → search your gene (e.g. `AT5G17420` CESA7, `AT5G12870` MYB46) → **Plant eFP**: record the **highest organ** + colour scale.
2. Switch to **Tissue-Specific Root eFP** → record **which root cell type** it switches on in (secondary-cell-wall genes → the **xylem**, where thick lignified walls are built).
3. **PEO** ([peo.ku.dk](https://peo.ku.dk/)) → same locus → record **(a)** highest organ (agrees with ePlant?); **(b)** the **top 3 co-expressed genes** (how many SCW genes do you already recognise — other `CESA`s, `IRX9`, `COBL4`, `KNAT7`, `MYB46/83`?); **(c)** the **protein domain** (PFAM/InterPro) + **MapMan biological function** — the annotation skills from the database & sequence modules.

> ✅ *Expected:* SCW genes peak in **inflorescence/mature stem** and localise to root **xylem**; PEO's top-co-expressed list is full of SCW genes; the domain matches the family (cellulose-synthase domain for a CESA, MYB DNA-binding domain for MYB46, laccase for LAC4). **A decoy (CAB1 / RBCS1A) breaks every expectation** — it peaks in *leaves*, its co-expressed list is *photosynthesis* genes, its domain is unrelated to walls; spotting that mismatch *is* the exercise.
> **→ Dossier:** domain · MapMan function · highest organ · root cell type · top 3 co-expressed.

### D2 — Who does my gene work with — and does it replicate?  *(CoNekT + ATTED-II)*

1. **CoNekT** ([conekt.ku.dk](https://conekt.ku.dk/)) → your gene → open its **co-expression neighbourhood**; list the SCW neighbours you recognise (e.g. CESA4 `AT5G44030`, CESA8 `AT4G18780`, IRX9 `AT2G37090`, COBL4 `AT5G15630`, KNAT7 `AT1G62990`, MYB46 `AT5G12870`).
2. **ATTED-II** ([atted.jp](https://atted.jp/)) → search box → same locus → read its co-expressed list.
3. **Cross-check (the point):** how many of **your Stage-4 top-5** (your atlas) reappear in CoNekT and/or ATTED-II (two *independent* atlases)? Record the overlap **/ 5**.

> ✅ *Expected:* both return a tight SCW module; for a CESA, the strongest partners **CESA4 + CESA8** recur across atlases. Reproducibility across independent datasets is the signal.
> **→ Dossier:** top 3 databases co-expressed · overlap with your Stage-4 list.

### D3 — From correlation to mechanism: PPI vs regulation  *(STRING + CoNekT)* — the centrepiece

1. **STRING** ([string-db.org](https://string-db.org/)) → your gene, organism *Arabidopsis thaliana*, required score **medium (0.400)** → which neighbours appear, and by **which evidence channel** (experiments / co-expression / text-mining)? Record the **physical (experiment-backed) partners**.
2. **CoNekT** → look up the regulators **MYB46** `AT5G12870`, **MYB83** `AT3G08500`, **SND1/NST3** `AT1G32770`, **KNAT7** `AT1G62990`. Which one sits **directly above your gene** in the secondary-wall hierarchy? Record it.

> ✅ *Expected:* the CESA4/7/8 triangle is recovered by **experiments**, but **MYB46→CESA links appear only via co-expression / text-mining — never physical experiments**, because a TF binds its target's **DNA**, not its protein. That gap is exactly why Week 2 adds a curated **regulatory** layer. *(The DNA motif MYB46 binds in those promoters is the **SMRE / M46RE** element — it reappears in the Week-2 exam.)*
> **→ Dossier:** PPI partners · the regulator directly above your gene.

### D4 — Cross-database consistency  *(PEO + EBI Expression Atlas + CoNekT)*

1. **PEO** ([peo.ku.dk](https://peo.ku.dk/)) → your gene → top organ.
2. **EBI Expression Atlas** → `ebi.ac.uk/gxa/genes/<your AGI>` (e.g. [ebi.ac.uk/gxa/genes/AT5G17420](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/gxa/genes/AT5G17420)) → Baseline → top tissue.
3. **CoNekT** → your gene → specificity → top organ.
4. Fill a 3-row table: *database | top organ | agrees?*

> ✅ *Expected:* all three converge on **stem**; the second-ranked organs may differ.
> **→ Dossier:** "top organ — agrees across 3 DBs? yes / no".

> **Demo only (not assessed):** the **EBI Single-Cell Atlas** ([ebi.ac.uk/gxa/sc](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/gxa/sc)) shows SCW genes restricted to xylem cell clusters; **Genevestigator** needs free academic registration → projector-only.

### Exam-style questions — the database block  *(assessed; 2025-exam style, point-scored)*

Answer in 2–4 sentences. The model answers — and their points — match how the exam is graded.

**Q1 (4 p) — Specificity of evidence.** Your gene and a transcription factor both peak in the mature stem **and** both localise to the xylem in the root eFP. (a) Why is shared tissue *and* shared cell type stronger evidence of a functional link than shared tissue alone? (b) Give one way two genes could share a tissue yet **not** be functionally related.

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (4 p)</summary>(a) The more <i>specific</i> the shared context, the lower the chance of coincidence — co-localising at cell-type resolution rules out the trivial "both are just stem-abundant" explanation, so the guilt-by-association is stronger (2 p). (b) Housekeeping / broadly high genes are abundant everywhere; or both genes respond independently to the same cue (e.g. both stress-induced) without working together (2 p).</details>

**Q2 (4 p) — Reproducibility across atlases.** Three of your Stage-4 top co-expressed genes reappear in CoNekT/ATTED-II; one does not. (a) What does cross-atlas reproducibility tell you that a single atlas cannot? (b) Give two distinct reasons a *real* partner could be missing from one atlas's list.

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (4 p)</summary>(a) Recurrence across <i>independent</i> datasets argues the correlation is genuine biology, not a batch/condition artefact of one experiment (2 p). (b) Any two of: different tissues/conditions sampled (the partnership is condition-specific); different normalization / network method / correlation cutoff; the gene is absent from that database's genome version or was filtered out (2 p).</details>

**Q3 (5 p) — PPI vs co-expression vs regulation (core concept).** In STRING the CESA4/7/8 are joined by *experiments*, but MYB46 links to its targets only by *co-expression / text-mining*. (a) Mechanistically, why does a TF have **no** physical-interaction edge to the genes it controls? (b) Which database type *can* draw the MYB46→CESA edge, and what experiment supports it? (c) What general rule about reading a single database follows?

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (5 p)</summary>(a) A TF binds its target gene's <b>promoter DNA</b>, not the target's protein; PPI assays (Y2H, AP-MS) detect protein–protein contact, so a regulatory TF→target link is invisible to them (2 p). (b) A curated <b>GRN</b> edge (ChIP/EMSA-backed): ChIP-seq/EMSA showing MYB46 binds the SMRE/M46RE elements in the CESA promoters, plus transactivation (2 p). (c) Each database reports only <b>one kind</b> of evidence; absence in one (e.g. PPI) is not absence of a relationship — you must combine layers (1 p).</details>

**Q4 (3 p) — Reading a disagreement.** Three databases agree your gene's top organ is stem but rank the 2nd/3rd organs differently. (a) Which disagreement matters more for your conclusion, and why? (b) What property of the underlying data most often explains rank shuffling low down the list?

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (3 p)</summary>(a) The <b>top-organ agreement</b> carries the biological conclusion (where the gene acts); the low-expression rank order sits near the detection floor and is noisy, so reshuffling there is expected and harmless (2 p). (b) Different sample sets / conditions (and sequencing depth / normalization) between atlases (1 p).</details>

**Q5 (4 p) — Synthesis.** (a) In one sentence, state your gene's function and name the **three independent evidence types** from your dossier that support it. (b) Which of those evidence types is the **weakest** for *proving* the function, and why?

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (4 p)</summary>(a) One defensible claim + three correctly-typed evidence lines — e.g. <i>"CESA7 is a secondary-wall cellulose synthase acting in stem xylem"</i>: <b>expression</b> (stem/xylem peak across ePlant + PEO + EBI), <b>co-expression</b> (the CESA4/8 module reproduced in your atlas + PEO + CoNekT), <b>physical/regulatory</b> (CESA triangle by STRING experiments + a MYB46→CESA regulatory edge) — 3 p, 1 per correctly-typed type. (b) <b>Co-expression is weakest</b> — it is only correlation (guilt by association), consistent with but not proof of a functional link; a physical (experiment-backed PPI) or a ChIP/EMSA-backed regulatory edge is far stronger (1 p).</details>

> **✍️ Dossier:** your completed table **and** the five answers are the **database cross-check** graded at the conference (rubric criterion 3).

---

## 📝 Week 1 — exam-style questions  *(assessed; 2025-exam style, point-scored)*

Close the week the way the exam tests it. Answer in 2–4 sentences; the model answers and their points match the grading. *(These cover Stages 1–4; the **database block** above carries its own five questions — together they are the Week-1 exam set.)*

**W1·Q1 (4 p) — Why normalize.** Raw counts can't be compared (a) between two *genes* in one sample, nor (b) for one *gene* between two samples. Give the confound in each case and the TPM step that removes it.

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (4 p)</summary>(a) <b>Gene length</b> — a longer gene collects more reads at equal expression; divide each count by gene length in kb (the RPK step) (2 p). (b) <b>Sequencing depth</b> — samples differ in total reads; scale each sample so its values sum to 1,000,000 (the per-million step) (2 p).</details>

**W1·Q2 (4 p) — The artefact normalization can't fix.** One gene (`Gene8`) had a huge count in `Root2`. (a) Why did TPM fail to remove the distortion — and even push the *other* genes in `Root2` down? (b) How did PCA reveal it, and why did removing the gene + re-normalizing repair the clustering?

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (4 p)</summary>(a) TPM forces every sample to sum to 1e6, so one runaway gene eats most of `Root2`'s budget and <b>deflates every other gene</b> in that column — a composition effect scaling cannot undo (2 p). (b) In PCA `Root2` separated from the other roots; dropping `Gene8` and re-normalizing restored each sample's true composition, so samples clustered by tissue (2 p).</details>

**W1·Q3 (4 p) — z-score & clustering.** (a) Why z-score each gene before clustering samples? (b) Your three clusters match root/leaf/pollen — what would it mean if samples instead clustered by *sequencing run*, and what would you do?

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (4 p)</summary>(a) Per-gene z-scoring compares each gene's <b>pattern</b>, not its magnitude, so highly expressed genes don't dominate the distance and weakly expressed but informative genes still count (2 p). (b) That is a <b>batch effect</b> — technical variation outweighs biology; correct for batch (a batch term in DESeq2, or ComBat) before interpreting (2 p).</details>

**W1·Q4 (4 p) — Co-expression = guilt by association.** (a) What does a tight co-expression neighbourhood let you infer about your gene, and what does it *not*? (b) Your top neighbour has PCC 0.97. Give **two distinct** explanations that produce this *without* the two genes physically interacting, and say why correlation alone cannot tell "A regulates B" from "a third factor drives both".

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (4 p)</summary>(a) A shared expression pattern is a <b>hypothesis</b> that the genes share a process or regulator (1 p); it is <b>not</b> proof of physical interaction or direct regulation — correlation ≠ causation (1 p). (b) Two of: a shared upstream regulator switches both on; both are specific to the same tissue; both respond independently to the same condition (e.g. stress). Correlation is <b>symmetric and unsigned</b>, so it cannot distinguish "A→B", "B→A" or "C→both" — that needs directed / perturbation evidence (2 p).</details>

**W1·Q5 (4 p) — PCA & the scree plot.** Your scree plot shows PC1 = 62 %, PC2 = 21 %. (a) What does each bar mean, and can you trust the 2-D PCA picture here? (b) What would it mean if all the bars were roughly equal height? (c) *Before* you removed Gene8, why did Root2 sit apart on PC1?

<details><summary>✅ Model answer (4 p)</summary>(a) Each bar = the share of total variance that component captures; PC1 + PC2 = 83 %, so the 2-D plot is a <b>faithful</b> summary — most structure lives in two dimensions (2 p). (b) Variance spread evenly across many PCs → the data is genuinely high-dimensional, no 2-D plot represents it, and you should distrust any clustering read off it (1 p). (c) The Gene8 spike — and TPM's deflation of every other gene in Root2 — made Root2's whole profile an outlier, so PC1 captured that artefact rather than tissue (1 p).</details>

> **✍️ Dossier:** keep your answers — the reasoning here (why TPM, artefact vs batch, PCA trust, correlation vs causation) is exactly what the conference Q&A probes.

---

# Week 2 — uge 44 · gene-regulatory networks

## The MYB46 gene-regulatory network  *(R + Cytoscape)*

You take **your adopted gene's** co-expression neighbourhood, overlay **physical** interactions (BioGRID) and **curated regulatory** edges (a literature-curated table — every edge carries a reference), and assemble the secondary-cell-wall regulatory cascade around **MYB46** in Cytoscape. The lesson you'll see for yourself: **MYB46 has no physical-interaction edges at all** — only a *regulatory* layer can connect a master TF to its targets (**N4e** explains why).

Each build step (**N1 → N2 → N3**) now **draws the network right in the page**, so you watch it grow layer by layer — co-expression, then physical (PPI), then regulatory (GRN) — before you export the polished figure to Cytoscape.

### N0 — (Re)claim your gene

webR forgets everything when the page reloads, so **set your gene here** before the network steps — whether you're continuing from Week 1 or starting a fresh session. Same pool as Stage 3: `CESA4 AT5G44030` · `CESA7 AT5G17420` · `CESA8 AT4G18780` · `IRX9 AT2G37090` · `LAC4 AT2G38080` · `MYB46 AT5G12870` · `SND1 AT1G32770` · `COBL4 AT5G15630`.

```{webr}
MY_GENE <- "______"   # <- your gene's AGI locus, e.g. "AT5G17420"
MY_NAME <- "______"   # <- a short name,       e.g. "CESA7"

say(grepl("^AT[1-5CM]G[0-9]{5}$", MY_GENE) && MY_GENE %in% rownames(EXPR),
    paste("adopted", MY_NAME, "(", MY_GENE, ") — ready for the network."),
    "pick a gene from the pool above (e.g. MY_GENE <- \"AT5G17420\", MY_NAME <- \"CESA7\") and re-run.")
```

### N1 — Build your co-expression network

You *ranked* your gene's neighbours in **Week 1 (Stage 4)**; webR forgets on reload, so we rebuild that ranking here and then turn it into a **network** — drawing an edge for **every pair of genes** (your gene *and* its neighbours) whose profiles correlate above a cutoff. So the neighbours connect to **each other**, not just to your gene. *(Run N0 first so `MY_GENE` is set.)*

```{webr}
HUB    <- MY_GENE                                  # your adopted gene (from N0)
target <- EXPR[HUB, ]                              # its expression across every organ
pcc    <- apply(EXPR, 1, function(r) suppressWarnings(cor(r, target)))  # (you filled this blank in Week 1)
nbhd   <- data.frame(gene = rownames(EXPR), symbol = sym[rownames(EXPR)],
                     description = desc[rownames(EXPR)], PCC = round(pcc, 3))
nbhd   <- head(nbhd[order(-nbhd$PCC), ], 40)       # top-40 neighbours (your gene is row 1)
print(head(nbhd, 12))
nb <- nbhd$gene                                    # the neighbour ids

# turn the neighbourhood into a NETWORK: keep every gene-PAIR correlated above a cutoff
C  <- cor(t(EXPR[nb, ]))                            # gene × gene correlation AMONG the neighbours
ut <- upper.tri(C)                                 # each pair once (not the diagonal)
hist(C[ut], breaks = 20, col = "grey85", xlab = "Pearson r between neighbour pairs",
     main = "Where do the strong edges start?")    # look for where the right-hand cluster begins
MY_CUTOFF <- ______                                # <- a correlation cutoff you justify, e.g. 0.85
keep  <- ut & C > MY_CUTOFF
edges <- data.frame(source = rownames(C)[row(C)[keep]],
                    target = colnames(C)[col(C)[keep]], type = "coexpression")

cat("Co-expression network:", length(unique(c(edges$source, edges$target))),
    "genes,", nrow(edges), "edges (cutoff", MY_CUTOFF, ")\n")
cynet(edges, hub = HUB, id = "cy_n1", title = paste(MY_NAME, "co-expression network (Layer 1)"))

say(nrow(edges) > 0,
    "co-expression network built — neighbours are linked to each other, not just to your gene.",
    "no edges yet — fill MY_CUTOFF (e.g. 0.85), and if still empty lower it to 0.8 and re-run.")
```

> **🔬 The cutoff is a knob.** The histogram above usually shows a bulk of weak pair-correlations and a separate cluster of strong ones on the right — a defensible cutoff sits **in the valley between them** (often ~0.85). Re-run with `MY_CUTOFF <- 0.95` (fewer, tighter edges) and `0.75` (a denser hairball). A tight module like the secondary-wall genes stays connected even at a high cutoff — that density *is* the biological signal.

### N2 — Add the physical layer (BioGRID PPI)

Your co-expression edges (N1) say "these genes behave alike." Now overlay **physical** protein–protein interactions from BioGRID — where two neighbours actually *touch*. *(Run N1 first so `edges` and `nb` exist.)*

```{webr}
edges <- edges[edges$type == "coexpression", , drop = FALSE]   # start fresh from your N1 co-expression edges
# overlay BioGRID physical interactions where BOTH partners are in your neighbourhood
pp <- ppi[ppi$source %in% nb & ppi$target %in% nb, c("source","target")]
if (nrow(pp) > 0) { pp$type <- "PPI"; edges <- rbind(edges, pp) }

cesa <- c("AT5G44030","AT5G17420","AT4G18780")     # the secondary-wall CESA triangle
cesa_ppi <- sum(edges$type == "PPI" & edges$source %in% cesa & edges$target %in% cesa)
cat("Edges now:", nrow(edges), " (PPI:", sum(edges$type == "PPI"),
    "; CESA–CESA physical edges:", cesa_ppi, ")\n")

# draw the network so far — Layers 1+2 (blue edges = physical PPI)
cynet(edges, hub = MY_GENE, id = "cy_n2", title = "Layers 1+2: co-expression (grey) + physical PPI (blue)")

say(exists("edges") && nrow(edges) > 0,
    "physical layer overlaid in blue. If CESA4/7/8 are in your neighbourhood, their PPI triangle appears.",
    "run N1 first so 'edges' and 'nb' exist, then re-run.")
```

### N3 — Add the curated regulatory layer

```{webr}
# the curated SCW regulatory edges — every one cites a paper + an experiment (ChIP/EMSA/transactivation)
print(grn[, c("source_symbol","target_symbol","interaction","evidence","reference")])

# turn them into typed edges: GRN_activates / GRN_represses / GRN_induces
grn_edges <- data.frame(source = grn$source_AGI, target = grn$target_AGI,
                        type = paste0("GRN_", grn$interaction))
all_edges <- rbind(edges, grn_edges)               # three layers now coexist

# the full network — Layers 1+2+3 (arrows = directed regulatory edges)
cynet(all_edges, hub = MY_GENE, id = "cy_n3",
      title = "Layers 1+2+3: + curated regulatory GRN (green = activates, red = represses)")

say(any(grepl("GRN", all_edges$type)) && any(all_edges$type == "GRN_represses"),
    "three layers stacked: co-expression, physical (PPI) and curated regulatory — including the KNAT7 ⊣ CESA8 brake. Every regulatory edge is reference-backed.",
    "make sure 'grn' loaded (it loads when the page opens) and that you stacked grn_edges.")
```

> Each regulatory edge carries a **`reference`** and an **`evidence` type** (ChIP / EMSA / transactivation), so the network is trustworthy by construction — you can point to the experiment behind every arrow.

### N4 — Read the hierarchy

```{webr}
out_deg <- sort(table(grn_edges$source), decreasing = TRUE)   # a regulator's number of targets
in_deg  <- sort(table(grn_edges$target), decreasing = TRUE)   # a target's number of regulators
cat("Top regulators (out-degree):\n");      print(head(out_deg, 3))
cat("\nMost-regulated genes (in-degree):\n"); print(head(in_deg, 3))

say(names(out_deg)[1] == "AT5G12870",
    "MYB46 (AT5G12870) has the highest out-degree — the hub of the cascade: NAC masters → MYB46/83 → biosynthesis genes.",
    "MYB46 (AT5G12870) should top the regulator list — run N3 first.")
```

> **🔑 Network vocabulary** (you'll use these in the cells and the exam):
> **degree** = number of edges at a node · **hub** = unusually high-degree node · **betweenness** = how many shortest paths run *through* a node (a high-betweenness node is a *bottleneck*) · **clustering coefficient** = do my neighbours link to *each other*? (≈1 = a tight clique/triangle; ≈0 = a *star*) · **component** = a connected piece of the graph · **articulation point (cut vertex)** = a node whose removal splits the graph · **scale-free** = a few hubs + many low-degree nodes (robust to random loss, fragile to losing a hub) · **feed-forward vs feedback** = an arrow path that goes only *downstream* (forward) vs one that loops *back* to an upstream node (feedback).

### N4b — Network metrics: don't just look, measure

Compute the network's structure (all base R, no packages): **degree** (how many connections), **betweenness** (how many shortest paths run *through* a node — the bottleneck), **clustering coefficient** (do my neighbours connect to each other?), shortest-path distance, and connected components. The functions below (`betweenness_base`, `bfs_dist`, `components_base`) are **black-box helpers** — you only need to *call* them and read the results, not follow every line. The headline: MYB46 is the **hub** (highest degree) *and* the **bottleneck** (highest betweenness) *and* a **star** (near-zero clustering) — every NAC signal reaches the biosynthesis genes only through it. The plot at the end renders all three metrics **on the network at once** — node **size** = degree, **fill** = betweenness, **border** = clustering — so MYB46 shows up big, red and thin-bordered (hub-bottleneck-star), while KNAT7 is small but thick-bordered (buried in a triangle).

```{webr}
## base-R network metrics (no igraph). grn_edges (source, target) exists from N3.
build_adj <- function(edges, directed = FALSE) {
  nodes <- sort(unique(c(edges$source, edges$target)))
  A <- matrix(0L, length(nodes), length(nodes), dimnames = list(nodes, nodes))
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(edges))) {
    A[edges$source[i], edges$target[i]] <- 1L
    if (!directed) A[edges$target[i], edges$source[i]] <- 1L
  }
  diag(A) <- 0L; A
}
A <- build_adj(grn_edges)                       # undirected adjacency

degree_undirected <- rowSums(A)                 # number of connections per node

bfs_dist <- function(A, src) {                  # shortest paths (hops) from one node
  nm <- rownames(A); dist <- setNames(rep(Inf, length(nm)), nm); dist[src] <- 0; q <- src
  while (length(q) > 0) { u <- q[1]; q <- q[-1]
    for (w in nm[A[u, ] == 1L]) if (is.infinite(dist[w])) { dist[w] <- dist[u] + 1; q <- c(q, w) } }
  dist
}
D <- t(sapply(rownames(A), function(s) bfs_dist(A, s)))
diameter <- max(D[is.finite(D)]); mean_spl <- mean(D[is.finite(D) & D > 0])

betweenness_base <- function(A) {               # Brandes' algorithm (unweighted)
  nm <- rownames(A); n <- length(nm); CB <- setNames(numeric(n), nm)
  for (s in nm) {
    S <- character(0); P <- setNames(vector("list", n), nm)
    sigma <- setNames(rep(0, n), nm); sigma[s] <- 1
    d <- setNames(rep(-1, n), nm); d[s] <- 0; q <- s
    while (length(q) > 0) { v <- q[1]; q <- q[-1]; S <- c(S, v)
      for (w in nm[A[v, ] == 1L]) {
        if (d[w] < 0) { d[w] <- d[v] + 1; q <- c(q, w) }
        if (d[w] == d[v] + 1) { sigma[w] <- sigma[w] + sigma[v]; P[[w]] <- c(P[[w]], v) } } }
    delta <- setNames(rep(0, n), nm)
    for (w in rev(S)) { for (v in P[[w]]) delta[v] <- delta[v] + (sigma[v]/sigma[w]) * (1 + delta[w])
      if (w != s) CB[w] <- CB[w] + delta[w] } }
  CB / 2                                          # undirected: halve (= Cytoscape "Analyze Network")
}
clustering_matrix <- function(A) {              # local clustering via triangle counting
  k <- rowSums(A); tri <- diag(A %*% (A %*% A)) / 2; denom <- k * (k - 1) / 2
  ifelse(denom > 0, tri / denom, 0)
}
components_base <- function(A) {                # connected components by flood-fill
  nm <- rownames(A); comp <- setNames(rep(NA_integer_, length(nm)), nm); k <- 0
  for (start in nm) if (is.na(comp[start])) { k <- k + 1; q <- start; comp[start] <- k
    while (length(q) > 0) { u <- q[1]; q <- q[-1]
      for (w in nm[A[u, ] == 1L]) if (is.na(comp[w])) { comp[w] <- k; q <- c(q, w) } } }
  comp
}
btw <- betweenness_base(A); cc <- clustering_matrix(A)
cat("Betweenness (undirected, halved = Cytoscape's value):\n"); print(round(sort(btw, decreasing = TRUE)[1:4], 1))
cat("Clustering coefficient:\n"); print(round(sort(cc, decreasing = TRUE)[1:4], 3))
cat(sprintf("Diameter = %d ; mean shortest path = %.2f ; components = %d\n",
            diameter, mean_spl, length(unique(components_base(A)))))

# ---- plot the metrics ON the interactive network: node SIZE = degree, FILL = betweenness, BORDER = clustering ----
nn   <- rownames(A)
nrm  <- function(v) if (diff(range(v)) > 0) (v - min(v)) / diff(range(v)) else rep(0.5, length(v))
heat <- colorRampPalette(c("#f7f7f7", "#fdae6b", "#e6550d", "#a50f15"))(24)   # pale → red
node_fill <- setNames(heat[1 + round(nrm(btw[nn]) * 23)], nn)        # fill = betweenness (the bottleneck)
node_size <- setNames(24 + 34 * nrm(degree_undirected[nn]), nn)      # node size (px) = degree
node_bord <- setNames(ifelse(cc[nn] >= 0.5, "purple", "#666666"), nn)# border colour = clustering
node_blwd <- setNames(1 + 6 * nrm(cc[nn]), nn)                       # border width (px) = clustering
cynet(grn_edges, hub = "AT5G12870", id = "cy_n4b",
      title = "GRN metrics — size: degree | fill: betweenness | border (purple/thick): clustering",
      node_fill = node_fill, node_size = node_size, node_border = node_bord, node_lwd = node_blwd)
cat("Encoding — size = degree; fill pale→red = betweenness (MYB46 reddest = bottleneck);",
    "border thick+purple = high clustering (KNAT7), thin = star-like (MYB46).\n")

say(names(which.max(btw)) == "AT5G12870",
    "MYB46 is BOTH the hub (top degree) AND the bottleneck (top betweenness, ~18x the next node) — confirm in Cytoscape's Analyze Network.",
    "expected MYB46 (AT5G12870) on top of betweenness — did grn_edges load (run N3)?")
```

> **The trap trio:** SND1, CESA8 and KNAT7 all have **degree 3**, yet betweenness 8.0 / 8.0 / 0.3 and clustering 0.333 / 0.333 / 0.667. Same degree, completely different roles — degree alone never tells you what a node *does*.

### N4c — Predict a perturbation (the directed GRN)

The GRN is **directed**, so it is a *predictive* instrument. **The sign rule:** a knockout removes an input, and the target moves **opposite** to the sign of the lost edge — lose an **activator** → target **DOWN**; lose a **repressor** → target **UP**; over-expression moves it *with* the sign. Multiply signs along a path; the prediction is clean only if every path carries the same net sign.

```{webr}
## trace targets and predict KO/OE outcomes from the directed signed GRN
sgn <- c(activates = 1L, induces = 1L, represses = -1L)
E   <- data.frame(from = grn$source_AGI, to = grn$target_AGI, s = sgn[grn$interaction],
                  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
nmof <- function(a) { s <- unname(sym[a]); ifelse(is.na(s), a, s) }   # AGI -> symbol (unnamed: no stray names)

targets_of <- function(g) {                                   # direct targets + sign
  d <- E[E$from == g, ]
  if (!nrow(d)) return("(none — this gene is a terminal effector)")
  data.frame(target = nmof(d$to), effect = ifelse(d$s > 0, "activates", "represses"), row.names = NULL)
}
predict_perturb <- function(g, mode = c("KO","OE")) {         # downstream prediction
  mode <- match.arg(mode); acc <- list()
  walk <- function(u, s, seen) {
    kids <- E[E$from == u, ]
    for (i in seq_len(nrow(kids))) { v <- kids$to[i]; sv <- s * kids$s[i]
      acc[[v]] <<- c(acc[[v]], sv); if (!(v %in% seen)) walk(v, sv, c(seen, v)) }
  }
  walk(g, 1L, g)
  if (!length(acc)) return("(terminal effector — KO/OE changes nothing downstream)")
  do.call(rbind, lapply(names(acc), function(v) {
    u <- unique(sign(acc[[v]])); netsign <- if (length(u) > 1) NA else u
    eff <- if (mode == "KO") -netsign else netsign
    data.frame(target = nmof(v),
               prediction = ifelse(is.na(eff), "AMBIGUOUS (conflicting paths)",
                                   ifelse(eff > 0, "UP", "DOWN")), row.names = NULL)
  }))
}
targets_of("AT5G12870")               # MYB46's direct targets
predict_perturb("AT1G62990", "KO")    # knat7 KO -> CESA8 UP (lose the repressor)
predict_perturb("AT5G12870", "KO")    # myb46 KO -> CESA4/CESA7 DOWN; CESA8 AMBIGUOUS (the feed-forward loop!)
predict_perturb("AT5G17420", "KO")    # cesa7 KO -> nothing downstream (it's a terminal effector)

# YOUR TURN — predict a knockout yourself. A regulator (e.g. MYB46 "AT5G12870", SND1 "AT1G32770")
# changes things downstream; a biosynthesis gene (a CESA) is a terminal effector and changes nothing.
my_ko <- "______"                     # <- e.g. "AT5G12870"
cat("Predicted effect of knocking out", nmof(my_ko), ":\n"); print(predict_perturb(my_ko, "KO"))
say(grepl("^AT[1-5CM]G[0-9]{5}$", my_ko),
    paste("KO prediction for", nmof(my_ko), "computed — explain each UP / DOWN / AMBIGUOUS using the sign rule."),
    "put a valid AGI locus in my_ko (e.g. \"AT5G12870\" for MYB46) and re-run.")
```

> **Why CESA8 is "ambiguous":** MYB46 **activates** CESA8 directly **and** activates KNAT7, which **represses** CESA8 — an **incoherent feed-forward loop** (a pulse generator that limits overshoot, *not* negative feedback: nothing loops back to MYB46). That's why *myb46* and *knat7* mutants move CESA8 in opposite directions. *(KNAT7 is a genetic negative regulator — knat7 mutants make thicker fibre walls but thinner, irregular vessel walls — so we model its net effect on CESA8 as repression: knat7 KO → CESA8 up, Li 2012. The snd1 single mutant is near wild-type — NAC redundancy; the strong loss needs the snd1 nst1 double, which spares vessels.)*
>
> **If your adopted gene is IRX9 or COBL4** (downstream biosynthesis genes not in the curated GRN table), you can't trace "what regulates *me*" directly — instead trace the **CESA module** it sits in (MYB46 → CESA4/7/8) and note your gene as a co-regulated member. Don't invent an edge: a missing edge is honest data, not a gap to fill.

### N4d — Topology & robustness

The degree distribution (one hub, many leaves) is **scale-free-like**: such networks are **robust to random failure** but **fragile to targeted attack on the hub** (lethality–centrality). On **log-log axes** a true power law (scale-free) shows up as a roughly **straight line** — and the hub sits as one lonely point far to the lower-right (high degree, only one such node). And raising the co-expression cutoff `MY_CUTOFF` steadily breaks the network into more, smaller pieces.

```{webr}
## degree distribution on LOG-LOG axes — a power law (scale-free) is a straight line
tot_deg <- rowSums(A); dd <- table(as.integer(tot_deg))
kk <- as.integer(names(dd)); ff <- as.integer(dd)            # degree k and how many nodes have it
plot(kk, ff, log = "xy", pch = 19, col = "steelblue", cex = 1.6,
     xlab = "degree k (log scale)", ylab = "number of nodes (log scale)",
     main = "GRN degree distribution — log-log (scale-free ≈ straight line)")
if (length(kk) > 1) abline(lm(log10(ff) ~ log10(kk)), col = "grey60", lty = 2)   # power-law guide line
text(kk, ff, labels = paste0("k=", kk), pos = 3, cex = 0.7, col = "grey40")

## lethality-centrality: which single deletion fragments the network?
remove_node <- function(A, v) { keep <- setdiff(rownames(A), v); A[keep, keep, drop = FALSE] }
frag <- sapply(rownames(A), function(g) length(unique(components_base(remove_node(A, g)))))
cat(sprintf("Intact: %d component. Remove MYB46 -> %d components. Remove any other -> 1.\n",
            length(unique(components_base(A))), frag["AT5G12870"]))

## how the co-expression network fragments as the cutoff rises (C is the neighbour-correlation matrix from N1)
if (!exists("C")) stop("Run N1 first so C (the neighbour-correlation matrix) exists.")
sweep_cut <- function(C, cutoffs = c(0.70, 0.80, 0.85, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99)) {
  do.call(rbind, lapply(cutoffs, function(cut) {
    Ac <- (C > cut) * 1L; diag(Ac) <- 0L
    lab <- components_base(`dimnames<-`(Ac, list(rownames(C), rownames(C))))
    data.frame(cutoff = cut, edges = sum(Ac)/2, components = length(unique(lab)))
  }))
}
print(sweep_cut(C))

say(frag["AT5G12870"] > 5,
    sprintf("removing MYB46 shatters the GRN into %d components; removing any other single gene leaves 1 — the hub is the only consequential cut-vertex.", frag["AT5G12870"]),
    "removing MYB46 should fragment the network far more than removing any other node.")
```

### N4e — What the three edge types mean

An edge isn't just a line — its meaning is fixed by **what it represents, whether it's directed, and what experiment made it**. Only the GRN layer licenses a knockout prediction.

| | **Co-expression** | **PPI (BioGRID)** | **GRN (regulatory)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Edge means | mRNA levels co-vary | proteins physically interact | a TF controls a target's transcription |
| Directed? | **No** | **No** | **Yes** (TF → target) |
| Sign | ± correlation | none | + activates / − represses |
| Predict a knockout? | no (correlative) | no (physical) | **yes** (causal + directed) |
| Confirmed by | RNA-seq correlation | Y2H, AP-MS | ChIP, EMSA, transactivation |

Causal ranking: **GRN > PPI > co-expression** — *co-expression finds candidates, PPI shows partnerships, GRN proves control.* Two paradoxes from your own data: **(1)** MYB46 *regulates* CESA7 but has **zero PPI edges** — a TF binds its target's DNA, not its protein, so the absence is informative, not a gap. **(2)** CESA4/7/8 are linked in **all three** layers (co-expression + PPI triangle + shared MYB46 regulator) — three independent assays converging is far stronger than any one edge.

### N4f — Interactive network viewer

**Drag the nodes, scroll to zoom.** This is the curated **MYB46 secondary-cell-wall GRN** (the same directed edges you loaded in N3), laid out as a cascade: **NAC master switches** at the top → **MYB46 / MYB83** (the hubs, drawn larger) → downstream **MYB/KNAT/C3H14** TFs → **biosynthesis genes** (CESA, LAC4) at the bottom. Green arrows = activation; the **red ⊣ edge** is the one repression (**KNAT7 ⊣ CESA8**); the dashed edge is MYB46→MYB85 (induced). *Find your adopted gene and trace what regulates it and what it regulates.*

```{=html}
<div id="grn-cy" style="width:100%;height:520px;border:1px solid #cbd5e0;border-radius:8px;background:#fff;margin:6px 0"></div>
<div style="font:12px system-ui,sans-serif;color:#4a5568;margin-bottom:10px">
  <span style="color:#dd8452">●</span> NAC master &nbsp;
  <span style="color:#c44e52">●</span> MYB46/83 hub &nbsp;
  <span style="color:#f2c08a">●</span> downstream TF &nbsp;
  <span style="color:#55a868">●</span> biosynthesis &nbsp;|&nbsp;
  <span style="color:#7faf7f">→</span> activates &nbsp; <span style="color:#c0392b">⊣</span> represses
</div>
<script src="cytoscape.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  var drawTries = 0;
  function draw(){
    if (typeof cytoscape === "undefined") {
      if (++drawTries > 40) {            // ~6 s: cytoscape.js never loaded
        var b = document.getElementById("grn-cy");
        if (b) b.innerHTML = '<div style="padding:16px;color:#a00;font:13px system-ui">⚠ Could not load the network viewer (cytoscape.js). Try a page reload, or view the same GRN interactively in N1–N3.</div>';
        return;
      }
      setTimeout(draw, 150); return;
    }
    var el = document.getElementById("grn-cy"); if (!el || el.dataset.done) return; el.dataset.done = "1";
    var tier = { SND1:0,NST1:0,NST2:0,VND6:0,VND7:0, MYB46:1,MYB83:1,
      MYB58:2,MYB63:2,MYB85:2,MYB52:2,MYB54:2,MYB103:2,C3H14:2,KNAT7:2,
      CESA4:3,CESA7:3,CESA8:3,LAC4:3 };
    var col = function(n){ return tier[n]===0?"#dd8452": tier[n]===1?"#c44e52": tier[n]===2?"#f2c08a":"#55a868"; };
    var E = [["SND1","MYB46","act"],["SND1","MYB83","act"],["NST1","MYB46","act"],["NST2","MYB46","act"],
      ["VND6","MYB46","act"],["VND7","MYB46","act"],["MYB46","CESA4","act"],["MYB46","CESA7","act"],
      ["MYB46","CESA8","act"],["MYB83","CESA8","act"],["MYB46","MYB58","act"],["MYB46","MYB63","act"],
      ["MYB46","MYB85","ind"],["MYB46","MYB52","act"],["MYB46","MYB54","act"],["MYB46","MYB103","act"],
      ["MYB46","C3H14","act"],["MYB46","KNAT7","act"],["SND1","KNAT7","act"],["MYB58","LAC4","act"],
      ["MYB63","LAC4","act"],["KNAT7","CESA8","rep"]];
    var nd = {}; E.forEach(function(e){ nd[e[0]]=1; nd[e[1]]=1; });
    var els = Object.keys(nd).map(function(n){ return {data:{id:n, c:col(n)}}; })
      .concat(E.map(function(e){ return {data:{source:e[0], target:e[1], kind:e[2]}}; }));
    cytoscape({
      container: el, elements: els, wheelSensitivity: 0.25,
      style: [
        {selector:"node", style:{ "label":"data(id)","background-color":"data(c)","width":34,"height":34,
          "font-size":11,"text-valign":"center","color":"#111","text-outline-width":2,"text-outline-color":"#fff" }},
        {selector:"#MYB46,#MYB83", style:{ "width":50,"height":50,"font-weight":"bold","font-size":13 }},
        {selector:"edge", style:{ "width":2,"curve-style":"bezier","target-arrow-shape":"triangle",
          "line-color":"#7faf7f","target-arrow-color":"#7faf7f","arrow-scale":1.1 }},
        {selector:"edge[kind='ind']", style:{ "line-style":"dashed" }},
        {selector:"edge[kind='rep']", style:{ "line-color":"#c0392b","target-arrow-color":"#c0392b",
          "target-arrow-shape":"tee","width":3 }} ],
      layout:{ name:"breadthfirst", directed:true, roots:"#SND1,#NST1,#NST2,#VND6,#VND7", spacingFactor:1.15, padding:12 }
    });
  }
  if (document.readyState === "loading") document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", draw); else draw();
})();
</script>
```

### N5 — Export for Cytoscape & interpret

*(Functional enrichment is an optional extension — see the **Appendix** at the end of this page.)*

```{webr}
nodes <- unique(c(all_edges$source, all_edges$target))
write.csv(data.frame(id = nodes, symbol = sym[nodes]), "myb46_nodes.csv", row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(all_edges, "myb46_edges.csv", row.names = FALSE)
cat("Wrote myb46_nodes.csv and myb46_edges.csv — download them from the file panel and import into Cytoscape.\n")
```

**Cytoscape walkthrough (printed run-sheet):** (1) *Import → Network from File* → `myb46_edges.csv`, map `source`/`target`/`type`. (2) *Import → Table from File* → `myb46_nodes.csv`, key = `id`. (3) **Style:** edge colour by `type` — co-expression grey, PPI blue, `GRN_activates` green arrow, `GRN_represses` red T-bar, `GRN_induces` dashed; node colour by role (regulator orange, biosynthesis green); diamond shape for the NAC master switches; node size ∝ GRN out-degree. (4) **Layout** hierarchically on the GRN edges → a top-to-bottom cascade. (5) *Tools → Analyze Network* → confirm MYB46 has the highest betweenness. (6) Export PDF/PNG for your dossier.

**✍️ Dossier (assessed) — close it out:**

1. **Convergence:** how many NACs point at MYB46? Are they the fibre set (SND1/NST1/NST2) or vessel set (VND6/VND7)?
2. **Hub:** is MYB46 the top out-degree *and* the highest-betweenness node? What does that mean biologically?
